# What the Architect Owns {#sec-what-architect-owns}

::: {.epigraph}
> *"The architect's two most important tools are the eraser at the drafting board and the wrecking bar at the site."*
>
> — Frank Lloyd Wright, *New York Times* (1953)
:::

::: {.column-margin}
**Author's Note:** Frank Lloyd Wright, the iconic American pioneer of organic architecture, recognized that the power to destroy and revise is just as important as the power to create. For us, this summarizes the conclusion of the book. AI systems and tools may generate candidates and automate routine checks, but accountable people or organizational roles must define the problem, decide what evidence supports which consequence, set the authority of automation, and answer for consequential commitments.
:::

```{=latex}
\abstract*{Architecture 2.0 changes the architect's work, but it does not make decision rights self-assigning. This chapter returns to the opening moonshot and asks what may be delegated, what must remain visibly assigned, and what shared infrastructure can make AI-assisted architecture studies comparable and cumulative. Models, tools, specialists, and automated checks may participate throughout a bounded study. Accountable people or organizational roles still define scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, and consequential commitment.}
```

::: {.callout-crux}
When AI systems, tools, and specialists can participate across a bounded
architecture study, which work may be delegated, which decision rights must
remain visibly assigned, and what must the field build so claims can be
compared and challenged?
:::

To answer those questions, consider the lighthouse prompt that opened the book. At the start of
the book, that prompt was a provocation.
It looked like a request for a future hardware foundation model[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model].
At this point, it should read differently. The prompt is not powerful because it is
short. It is powerful because it exposes the need for a bounded architecture
study that connects the request to alternatives, artifacts, tools, experiments,
explanations, and an accountable decision.

[^fn-arch-hardware-foundation-model]: The phrase names a tempting but incomplete reading of the lighthouse prompt, a single broadly trained hardware model that maps intent to designs. The missing object is a bounded architecture study grounded in representations, tools, experiments, evidence, challenge, and accountable judgment. When that work iterates, a design loop supplies its execution and analysis mechanism.

Such a study includes workload definition, architecture representations, tool
interfaces, method roles, feedback budgets, evidence records, challenge paths,
and accountable decisions. A design loop connects these elements when the work
requires repeated proposal, evaluation, rejection, and revision. Cards,
receipts, and checks help inspect the work, but the architectural finding or
decision remains the point. AI systems do not eliminate the computer architect.
They extend how architects formulate problems, construct alternatives, direct
tool-backed work, interpret evidence, and accept commitments.

## The Architect Is an Accountable Role

AI assistance broadens the architect's responsibility across the study, but not
every check has to move through one person's hands. Simulators, policy checks,
and regression suites may reject candidates or advance routine transitions
automatically. The accountable architect or organizational role defines the
scope of that automation, the authority each check carries, the evidence needed
for each class of decision, the escalation and waiver paths, and the point at
which a consequential commitment requires explicit acceptance. The work can be
distributed; answerability cannot disappear into the distribution.

In this chapter, *the architect* therefore names the accountable decision role,
not necessarily one individual performing every operation. Large programs may
assign workload scope, software compatibility, physical signoff, security, and
product acceptance to different authorized roles. The study remains governed
when those rights, handoffs, and limits are visible. This is not an argument for
one heroic generalist. It is an argument against authority becoming anonymous
as work crosses models, tools, teams, and abstraction levels.

That responsibility is especially sharp near silicon commitment. A generative
model or an automated optimizer is not answerable for a tapeout decision. Some software
systems are also hard to roll back, but tapeout makes the commitment unusually
durable. A bug committed to silicon can require a respin and substantial
schedule delay against a design investment that industry estimates put at
roughly \$725M at 2\ nm [@InternationalBusinessStrategies2024ChipDesignCost].
The 2024 Wilson Research Group study reports first-silicon success at 14
percent, so most projects already absorb at least one respin
[@Foster2025WilsonVerification2024]. A named tapeout or product role therefore has
to own the acceptance criteria, unresolved waivers, and commitment explicitly.
The architect may no longer craft every artifact by hand. The role still helps
formulate the architecture question, shape consequential alternatives,
interpret cross-layer effects, and review the evidence path that justifies
commitment while remaining answerable for the artifact's consequences.

::: {.callout-learning-objectives}
After this chapter you can assign accountable decision rights across an
AI-assisted architecture study. That means you can:

- **Distinguish** routine checks and transitions that may be automated from decision rights that must remain assigned and answerable;
- **Map** evidence to the consequence it can support without treating fidelity as authority;
- **Explain** why an accountable architect must understand delegated abstractions, tools, and evidence without personally executing every step;
- **Answer** the strongest objections to Architecture 2.0 without treating automation as ownership;
- **Identify** the datasets, environments, methods, evaluation practices, institutions, and educational outcomes needed for cumulative progress;
- **Test** a completed study against the six capabilities introduced in the Preface.
:::

## Return to the Moonshot

The lighthouse prompt makes these shifted responsibilities concrete:

> Design a low-power, 64-bit RISC-V-based compute subsystem for an
> XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload. Realize it as a vector-capable
> CPU, accelerator, or SoC block under a 3\ W TDP target in a 3\ nm-class LP
> mobile process, and return a design-space report with evidence and rejected
> alternatives.

This prompt may seem straightforward, but beneath the surface it binds together
workload, software, architecture, and physical constraints. For an AI-assisted
study to act on this prompt, these implicit contracts must become explicit
constraints and rejection criteria.

::: {.callout-lighthouse title="The prompt hides interface contracts"}
**Context.** The opening request is not a single hardware specification. It
bundles workload, software, architecture, technology, and evidence obligations
that must be made explicit before an automated participant can act on them.

**In the Lighthouse prompt.** "XRBench-class real-time mobile XR workload"
creates an obligation to specify a workload distribution, quality-of-experience
target, benchmark version, traces, and software stack. For an automated system, this workload
obligation has to be represented as machine-readable data, including scenario labels, trace
provenance, coverage limits, excluded cases, quality-of-experience labels, and
conditions for rejecting inputs outside the represented workload distribution.
"64-bit RISC-V-based" means the AI-assisted environment
needs access to correct compiler toolchains, simulators, and instruction semantics,
rather than just generating text that looks like RISC-V. The fragment "vector-capable CPU,
accelerator, or SoC block" defines the system's allowed action space. The environment
must be given explicit rules about which integration paths it can explore and
which interfaces are fixed.

**Evidence boundary.** "3\ W TDP target" and "3\ nm-class LP mobile process"
create power, thermal, and physical-design constraints; an optimizer evaluating
candidates must not confuse a fast proxy average-power estimate with the sustained
thermal or signoff evidence required for a final commitment.

**Takeaway.** The accountable decision role determines which contracts are
fixed, which are exposed as actions to the automated tool, which checks may
advance candidates automatically, and what evidence is sufficient for a
consequential commitment.
:::

Making evidence explicit exposes a hierarchy of commitment. As an
AI-assisted study explores candidates, it can draw on different levels of
fidelity, from fast, imprecise proxies to cycle-level simulation, RTL
verification, emulation, synthesis, place-and-route, and
signoff checks. The accountable role defines which evidence is required for
each proposed decision and may delegate that mapping through policy and
automated checks. Strong evidence does not oblige a stronger commitment, and no
amount of fidelity silently authorizes an irreversible action. Decision
consequences form the commitment ladder, while evidence sources remain a
separate axis mapped to each proposed decision (@fig-commitment-ladder).

![**Evidence and commitment are separate:** Four representative evidence-source families do not align one to one with the five commitment levels. Evidence differs in fidelity, cost, scope, and validation, while decisions differ in consequence and reversibility. The accountable mapping states the required evidence, challenge conditions, owner, and strongest supported commitment for each claim or decision. Stronger evidence does not authorize stronger commitment by itself.](images/F10-commitment-ladder){#fig-commitment-ladder width="100%" fig-alt="Two-column diagram separating evidence available to a bounded study from five commitment levels. Both feed an accountable mapping that states required evidence, challenge conditions, owner, and strongest supported commitment."}

The mapping must respect one more difference between the levels, because they
are not priced evenly. At the exploratory level,
swinging Wright's wrecking bar costs compute time, and a proxy-backed sweep of
several thousand configurations can be re-run in days
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Near silicon, the cited record stops pricing
individual reversals and instead prices what a reversal puts at stake, the
accumulated design investment (@fig-ch10-cost-of-reversal). That asymmetry,
not any general distrust of automation, is why decision rights concentrate at
the high-commitment end of the ladder.

```{python}
#| label: fig-ch10-cost-of-reversal
#| fig-cap: |
#|   **The wrecking bar grows more expensive with commitment:** Cited anchors for what reversing a decision involves at each commitment level of @fig-commitment-ladder. An exploratory reversal is a compute-only re-run; an ArchGym proxy sweep re-ran roughly 4,000 experiments in about two days [@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym]. Experimental rework is absorbed as verification effort, already a 50-60 percent median share of project schedule [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy]. No cited source prices a single implementation reversal or a mask set; the bands instead show the design investment that public node estimates put at risk, roughly $175M at 10 nm to $725M at 2 nm [@BauerEtAl2020SemiconductorDesignManufacturing; @InternationalBusinessStrategies2024ChipDesignCost]. That exposure is routine, since 76 percent of ASIC projects needed two or more spins in 2022 [@Foster2022WilsonVerificationStudy] and first-silicon success fell to 14 percent in 2024 [@Foster2025WilsonVerification2024]. Past deployment, Intel's Pentium FDIV recall took a $475M charge for one escaped defect [@IntelPentiumFDIV]. Bands are investment at risk, never per-event reversal prices.
#| out-width: "92%"
#| fig-alt: "Five-row chart of the commitment ladder levels against a logarithmic dollar axis. The exploratory and experimental rows carry text noting compute time and engineer time with no cited dollar figure. The implementation row shows a band from about 175 to 725 million dollars of design investment at risk, the integration row a band from about 590 to 725 million dollars exposed at a respin, and the irreversible row a single 475 million dollar recall-charge marker."

import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
from _python.arch2_plots import COLORS, add_note_box, apply_style, row_axis, top_log_axis

apply_style()

# Source receipt: data/source-receipts/chapter10-cost-of-reversal.csv
# Cited reversal anchors per commitment level, all from sources already in
# references.bib. Only the exploratory and irreversible levels have direct
# per-event anchors (an ArchGym proxy sweep of ~4,000 experiments re-ran in
# about two days; Intel's Pentium FDIV recall took a $475M charge). The
# implementation and integration bands are the design investment public node
# estimates put at risk ($175M at 10 nm to $725M at 2 nm; $590M/$725M at
# 3 nm/2 nm), never per-event reversal prices; no cited source prices a mask
# set, a respin, or a fleet rollback, so those rows carry text instead of
# fabricated bands.
M = 1e6
rows = [
    {"label": "Exploratory", "note": "re-run the search",
     "kind": "text",
     "text": "compute time only; a ~4,000-experiment proxy\nsweep re-ran in about two days (ArchGym)",
     "right": "no dollar\nfigure cited", "color": COLORS["muted"]},
    {"label": "Experimental", "note": "rework lands in verification",
     "kind": "text",
     "text": "engineer time; verification already takes a\n50-60% median share of project schedule",
     "right": "no dollar\nfigure cited", "color": COLORS["muted"]},
    {"label": "Implementation", "note": "late reversal risks the\ndesign investment",
     "kind": "band", "low": 175 * M, "high": 725 * M,
     "right": "~$175M-725M\nat risk", "color": COLORS["designspace"]},
    {"label": "Integration", "note": "respin exposes the investment;\nmask-set price uncited",
     "kind": "band", "low": 590 * M, "high": 725 * M,
     "right": "~$590M-725M\nexposed", "color": COLORS["methods"]},
    {"label": "Irreversible", "note": "field recall or rollback",
     "kind": "point", "low": 475 * M, "high": 475 * M,
     "right": "$475M\nrecall charge", "color": COLORS["constraints"]},
]

fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(4.9, 3.0))
fig.subplots_adjust(left=0.30, right=0.82, top=0.85, bottom=0.21)

row_axis(ax, len(rows))
top_log_axis(
    ax,
    xlim=(1.0 * M, 2500 * M),
    xticks=[1 * M, 10 * M, 100 * M, 1000 * M],
    xticklabels=["$1M", "$10M", "$100M", "$1B"],
    xlabel="cited dollar exposure when a decision is reversed",
)

for y, row in enumerate(rows):
    ax.axhline(y, color=COLORS["row"], linewidth=0.65, zorder=0)
    if row["kind"] == "band":
        ax.hlines(y, row["low"], row["high"], color=row["color"],
                  linewidth=2.2, zorder=2)
        ax.scatter([row["low"], row["high"]], [y, y], marker="s", s=17,
                   facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=row["color"],
                   linewidth=0.9, zorder=3)
    elif row["kind"] == "point":
        ax.scatter([row["low"]], [y], marker="s", s=26,
                   facecolor=COLORS["note_fill"], edgecolor=row["color"],
                   linewidth=1.1, zorder=3)
    else:
        ax.text(1.3 * M, y, row["text"], ha="left", va="center",
                fontsize=5.3, style="italic", color=COLORS["muted"], zorder=2)
    ax.text(-0.415, y - 0.17, row["label"],
            transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center",
            fontsize=6.6, fontweight="bold", color=COLORS["ink"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(-0.415, y + 0.26, row["note"],
            transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(), ha="left", va="center",
            fontsize=5.3, color=COLORS["muted"], clip_on=False)
    ax.text(1.03, y, row["right"], transform=ax.get_yaxis_transform(),
            ha="left", va="center", fontsize=5.9, fontweight="bold",
            color=row["color"], clip_on=False)

add_note_box(
    fig,
    "Bands are public design-investment estimates at risk in a late reversal, not per-event prices; no cited source prices a mask set, a respin, or a fleet rollback.",
    xywh=(0.14, 0.035, 0.72, 0.075),
    fontsize=5.5,
)

plt.show()
plt.close(fig)
```

The requested deliverable also matters. A design-space report is different
from an RTL artifact. RTL functional verification is different from a
placed-and-routed implementation that has passed timing and physical signoff.
Logical correctness alone does not establish routability or satisfaction of
timing, power-integrity, and thermal constraints. Each step changes the evidence
standard. The same prompt can support a brainstorming study, a
research prototype, a simulator-backed design-space exploration, or a
high-commitment implementation study. Treating all of those deliverables as
the same is one of the fastest ways to overclaim.

To avoid this overclaiming, the framework developed here turns the prompt into
the design-loop card's fields in question form (@sec-architecture-20-ontology,
@sec-appendix-b-design-loop-card), with the mechanism-account question added as
a review requirement rather than a card field:
What task is being solved? What representation is available? What assumptions
shape the execution mechanism? What artifact makes the claim testable? What
tools can the system act on? What method roles are allowed? What feedback is
affordable? What evidence would make the result credible? What architectural
mechanism should explain it, and what observation would contradict that
account? What alternatives were rejected? Who can stop or reopen the work? Who is
accountable for the decision, including any automated transition or waiver?

Together, those questions define the part of
the design space the architect exposes to methods, tools, and AI-assisted systems. Choosing
what to expose is an architectural act. Expose too little and the study cannot
find useful alternatives. Expose too much without constraints, evidence, and
challenge authority, and an automated participant can optimize the wrong object or cross a
commitment boundary before anyone understands what happened.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Check the Exposed Action Boundary"}
When defining an AI-assisted environment, check the boundary of exposed
actions. Has an accountable role provided explicit constraints, evidence
checks, and stop conditions for every knob the automated optimizer can turn? If
not, it may optimize an invalid metric and cross a commitment boundary
unnoticed. If L2 cache size is exposed as a tunable action, an area-budget check
can reject oversize configurations, while a congestion check can screen
candidates likely to be unroutable.
:::

Enforcing those boundaries marks the shift from prompt to bounded study. The
prompt motivates the work. The study makes the architecture question,
alternatives, tool path, evidence, explanation, and decision inspectable.
Prompt-to-chip asks whether a system can produce an impressive final artifact
from a sentence. The more durable test asks whether a bounded study preserves
enough of the claim-producing work for another architect to understand what the
artifact establishes. When the work iterates, a design loop is one mechanism
for preserving that path.

## Decision Rights Must Remain Assigned

Even the most explicit study leaves decision rights that cannot be orphaned.
Much of its execution can be delegated. Models can propose, simulators can measure,
formal tools can reject, policy engines can route routine transitions, and
specialists can review their domains. What must remain assigned are the
accountable rights named at the outset: scope, the automation envelope, evidence
sufficiency, escalation and waivers, and consequential commitment. These rights
may be distributed across authorized organizational
roles, but they cannot be transferred to a model as if the model could answer
for the organizational outcome.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Trace Delegated Authority"}
When splitting an architecture task across multiple automated participants,
trace each decision right. Which system or tool may halt or advance a routine
transition? Which observation requires escalation, who may grant a waiver, and
which accountable role accepts the consequential commitment? Adding more
participants must not make those assignments disappear.
:::

Failures in other high-stakes fields show the cost of independent challenge
arriving after consequential use.

::: {.callout-field-note title="Challenge After Consequential Use"}
In clinical genomics, published predictors were already being used to allocate
patients in clinical trials. Baggerly and Coombes reconstructed the analyses and
found reversed labels, duplicated and inconsistently labeled samples, and other
simple errors, some of which persisted through published corrections
[@BaggerlyCoombes2009].

**Lesson:** Independent challenge needs a defined route to reach an accountable decision before or during consequential use.
:::

That defined route is only credible when someone on the team understands the
machinery well enough to run it. Understanding that machinery is not the same as
personally executing every step. An architect need not write every harness, inspect every timing path, or
run every place-and-route job. The accountable team must retain named experts
who understand the relevant abstractions, tool contracts, evidence limits, and
likely failure modes well enough to set acceptance criteria, challenge
anomalous results, judge a waiver, and know when a stronger check is required.
A program that cannot produce someone who can explain what an automated check
establishes, what it misses, and why it may advance a claim cannot responsibly
delegate authority to that check. That requirement runs into a classic irony of
automation. Delegation removes the routine work that once trained the experts
the delegation still requires [@Bainbridge1983Ironies], which is why the
education agenda later in this chapter is a dependency of the framework rather
than an appendix to it.

This makes the accountable work explicitly cross-layer. A future architect may
not be only a CPU architect, accelerator architect, compiler specialist, or
physical-design expert. Specialists and automated systems can operate pieces of
the stack; accountable roles compose their decisions by determining which
hardware knobs may change, which software contracts remain fixed, which
workload scenarios matter, which checks carry authority, and which deployment
consequences are acceptable.

Leveson's central move in safety engineering was to treat safety as a property
of interactions in a sociotechnical control structure rather than the sum of
reliable components [@Leveson2012EngineeringSaferWorld]. The transfer here is
limited because most architecture studies are not safety cases. Still, a
capable model or a passing tool check cannot make an architectural claim
reviewable on its own. Reviewability arises from how a bounded study connects
scope, representations, tools, evidence, challenge, and assigned decision
rights.

This division separates delegated work from accountable decision rights
(@fig-architect-owned-boundary). Automated methods may represent state,
generate candidates, evaluate proxies, call tools, critique results, summarize
evidence, preserve provenance, and reject candidates under declared rules.
Those are substantial contributions. They may propose scope, evidence policies,
or escalation paths, but they cannot legitimately assign themselves
organizational authority, grant themselves waivers, or become answerable for
product risk. An automated optimizer might tune core parameters against a
thermal objective, for instance, while a package or
product role remains accountable for the rule that blocks a reliability or
certification violation and for any waiver that permits the design to advance.

![**Accountable decision rights remain visible across AI-assisted architecture work:** Within a bounded study, AI systems, tools, and specialists may assist formulation, exploration, implementation, evaluation, explanation, and defense. The architectural claim remains the unit of review, while decision rights for scope, abstraction, evidence, automation, exceptions, commitment, and reopening remain assigned.](images/F11-architect-owned-boundary){#fig-architect-owned-boundary width="100%" fig-alt="Bounded architecture study containing the six architecture capabilities, delegated assistance, an architectural claim for review, supporting instruments, and a full-width band of accountable decision rights."}

Decision rights span the whole study rather than attaching to any single
capability, and that distinction becomes a practical review object in
@tbl-architect-responsibilities. Semiconductor programs already run signoff
checklists at stage boundaries, but those gates govern the artifact. The rights
in the table govern the claim-producing work upstream of the gates, which is
where AI participation now concentrates and where existing signoff is silent.
The point is to assign decision rights and leave records that expose how
technical claims connect to organizational, economic, ethical, and deployment
consequences.

| **Decision right** | **Why accountable assignment matters** | **What may be automated or delegated** | **Inspectable record** |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| **Intent, scope, and constraints** | The study must serve a real objective rather than an available benchmark or proxy. | Elicit constraints, compare formulations, run policy checks, and reject declared violations. | Problem statement, non-goals, workload slice, hard constraints, and accountable role. |
| **Abstraction and representation** | Encoded state determines what participants can see, optimize, ignore, or simplify incorrectly. | Translate artifacts, organize traces, find gaps, and propose structured schemas. | Architecture schema, exposed design space, assumptions, hidden-state list, and redaction boundary. |
| **Evidence sufficiency** | Evidence must match the consequence, reversibility, and cost of being wrong. | Gather measurements, track provenance, estimate uncertainty, replay baselines, and route anomalies. | Tool and environment contracts, calibration records, evidence summary, validity scope, and escalation threshold. |
| **Authority, escalation, and waivers** | AI-assisted work needs declared rules for who or what may stop, advance, reopen, or override a decision. | Apply validated checks, reject invalid candidates, advance routine transitions, and notify reviewers. | Authority matrix, automated-check configuration, exception record, waiver rationale, expiration, and reviewer. |
| **Consequential commitment and answerability** | Product, tapeout, deployment, safety, security, cost, and lifecycle consequences need an answerable owner. | Summarize tradeoffs, check completeness, monitor drift, and trigger rollback or revalidation. | Decision note, accepted and rejected alternatives, residual risks, commitment level, ownership transfer, and reopening condition. |

: **Accountable decision rights become explicit study obligations:** Execution may be automated or delegated, while scope, authority, evidence sufficiency, escalation, waivers, consequential commitment, and answerability remain visibly assigned. {#tbl-architect-responsibilities tbl-colwidths="[18,26,27,29]"}

Read each row as a decision right whose work may be automated or delegated
while its inspectable record stays accountable. That boundary also clarifies the
word *agentic*. The book is not arguing
that every architecture study should become agentic or autonomous. Agentic
systems[^fn-ml-agentic-system] are useful because they can act on represented
study state, call tools, revise plans, use feedback, and coordinate method
roles. When work iterates, they may participate inside a design loop. Action is
still not accountability. As these systems become more capable, the authority
delegated to them and the accountable roles around them must become more
explicit.

[^fn-ml-agentic-system]: An AI system capable of pursuing complex goals over time, making its own decisions about which tools to use and what steps to take next. In this book such systems participate inside bounded architecture studies. Authority assigned to their actions does not make them answerable organizational decision owners.

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Trace the Decision"}
If an AI-assisted study proposes an invalid artifact, the review record
must show the assignment of accountability. Can you trace which person or
organizational role defined the scope, authorized automated checks, set the
evidence threshold, approved any waiver, and accepted the consequential
commitment? If those assignments disappear, the study has hidden the
architecture decision.
:::

## Eliciting and Codifying Tacit Knowledge

If accountable roles must define constraints and accept consequential risk,
those constraints still have to come from somewhere, and much of the time they
are not written down anywhere.

Engineering teams rely on knowledge that does not yet appear in formal
specifications, the tacit knowledge that Polanyi distinguished from knowledge
that can be fully stated [@Polanyi1966TacitDimension]. A packaging engineer may
know that a particular chiplet partition will break the power-delivery mesh. A
compiler lead may know that the current backend in LLVM, a compiler
infrastructure, cannot lower a proposed matrix layout. A senior architect may anticipate that an accelerator's
coherency traffic will starve the XR display controller. A local proxy may
expose none of these integration failures.

Experienced teams actively recover this context before acting. They find the
relevant domain owners, surface undocumented constraints, resolve conflicts,
and decide which parts are valid for the study at hand.

Any delegated participant is limited by the state it can observe and the
interactions it is allowed to use. Missing tacit constraints become
invalid-action and proxy-mismatch risks. An automated participant may stall,
ask for review, or optimize a candidate that violates packaging, compiler,
coherency, or display-timing constraints.

This forces a shift in the architect's daily work. An automated participant can
help interview experts, retrieve prior decisions, and propose candidate
constraints, but an authorized domain owner or review process must validate what
enters represented study state.
That validation bridges the gap between undocumented organizational wisdom and
represented study state (@fig-tacit-extraction-bridge).

Translating hallway conversations and senior-engineer judgment into environment
contracts and rejection conditions allows bounded automation to reject invalid
designs before they consume expensive simulation or implementation time.

An automated participant cannot treat an elicited statement as authority merely
because it can ask for one. The accountable process must resolve conflicts,
record scope and ownership, and explicitly bound the environment. In Architecture 2.0,
eliciting and validating tacit knowledge is an engineering step for bounded
automation. The record must also state when the knowledge expires, conflicts with another
domain, or requires revalidation, the conditions that send a record back
through accountable validation in @fig-tacit-extraction-bridge.

![**Tacit knowledge requires elicitation and accountable validation:** AI can help elicit and organize domain knowledge, but it has no direct authority path into represented study state. An accountable domain role validates constraints, assumptions, and rejection conditions, and conflicts, expiry, or new evidence trigger revalidation.](images/F10-tacit-extraction-bridge){#fig-tacit-extraction-bridge width="100%" fig-alt="Diagram showing domain knowledge passing through AI-assisted elicitation and accountable validation before entering represented study state, with conflict, expiry, and new evidence returning the record for revalidation."}

## The Strongest Objections

This insistence on visible accountable decision rights, and on representing the
tacit constraints that bound delegated action, is a strong stance. A field-defining
claim should survive its sharpest critics, so it is worth stating the strongest
objections plainly.

The first objection is that Architecture 2.0 is just good experimental
methodology with new names. Provenance, baselines, rejection criteria, and
held-out validation[^fn-ml-held-out-validation] are not new. That objection is
correct about the lineage. As @sec-moonshot argued, machine learning first
entered computer architecture inside mechanisms and bounded human-specified
methods [@ChenPenneyJimenez2020AIArchitecture], and those uses remain
important. The shift comes when AI also participates in producing the claim
about the design, not only in the design itself. More of the claim-producing
work can now be delegated, so its scope, transformations, evidence limits, and
decision authority must remain inspectable.

The book therefore borrows established forms deliberately. Its structured
evidence record draws from assurance cases and Goal Structuring Notation, a
graphical notation for assurance arguments [@Kelly2004GSN]. Automotive functional safety under ISO 26262 provides a
neighboring standards context [@ISO2018Iso26262]. The fidelity ladder draws from
multi-fidelity modeling[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity]
[@PeherstorferWillcoxGunzburger2018Multifidelity]. Naming these lineages
clarifies what Architecture 2.0 inherits and what computer architecture still
has to build for itself.

[^fn-ml-held-out-validation]: Lineage matters here. Held-out validation is one of the established rigor practices this book retains. Architecture 2.0 asks that the task split, withheld evidence, and scope of the resulting claim remain explicit when AI participates in the study.

[^fn-ml-multi-fidelity]: Here multi-fidelity modeling is the ancestor of the book's fidelity ladder, the architect-owned choice among cheap proxies and higher-cost evidence. The commitment boundary remains a separate decision about what that evidence authorizes.

The second objection is that AI for systems design is overhyped, and a flagship
result in learned macro placement is contested. That is true, and this book
treats it as evidence rather than embarrassment. The placement dispute in
@sec-feedback-verification-trust is exactly why the field needs reproducible, end-to-end benchmarks and
shared evaluation and adjudication. An honest framework predicts that some flagship
claims will need narrowing, stronger evidence, or shared adjudication; its value
is a standard that makes the difference visible.

The third objection is that the design-loop card is process bureaucracy that
will not survive a deadline. The card is not a mandatory form; it is a
compact index for studies where tools or automated participants can advance candidates faster than
reviewers can reconstruct the evidence. A short initial pass can reveal whether
a result rests on a proxy nobody validated or a baseline nobody documented. The
time required and the defects found should be measured in real review settings
rather than assumed.
The cost of skipping that check is paid later, at higher commitment, where it is
most expensive. The card earns its place only when it is shorter than the
mistake it prevents.

The fourth objection is that the framework is too conservative because it
retains accountable decision rights while the goal is to deploy more capable AI
systems. Architecture 2.0 does not oppose automation. Validated checks can
reject candidates, routine transitions can advance automatically, and
specialized reviews can be delegated. The requirement is that their scope and
authority be declared, exceptions escalate, and consequential commitments
remain answerable. This requirement does not depend on a permanent weakness in
current models. Technical competence and organizational authority are different
properties. The design principle is not less automation. It is evidence-matched
automation with visible decision rights.

These objections narrow the discipline without reducing it to current model
capabilities or one process instrument. A design loop is one execution and
analysis mechanism inside a bounded study when the work iterates. It is not the
field's intellectual endpoint.

## What the Field Must Build

Making one bounded study reviewable is a start, not a field. Architecture 2.0
becomes cumulative when tasks, environments, evaluations, and claims can be
compared across projects while decision rights remain visible, without
pretending that every artifact can be public. The required infrastructure is a
stack. Its layers have different maintainers and failure
modes, and progress at one layer cannot substitute for a missing layer above or
below it. Independent evaluation can sometimes examine protected artifacts
without turning them into a public warehouse, but that arrangement still needs
declared procedures, qualified evaluators, conflict rules, and a public account
of what the evaluation establishes.

@tbl-field-building-stack states the concrete build agenda. The resource
registry in @sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources remains the actionable
inventory of current projects, tools, datasets, venues, and missing resources;
this chapter states the properties that inventory should help the field
assemble.

| **Layer** | **What the field should build** | **What it enables** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Datasets, benchmarks, and paired multi-fidelity trajectories** | Versioned tasks, workload slices, candidate actions, proxy observations, stronger-tool observations, successful and failed outcomes, costs, and redaction metadata linked across stages. | Learning when a cheap signal transfers, when it reverses, and which failures remain local to a workload, tool, or technology context. |
| **Versioned environments and tool contracts** | Maintained harnesses with legal actions, observation schemas, failure semantics, provenance, dependency versions, and explicit proprietary boundaries. | Comparable method evaluation and diagnosis when a result changes because the tool path changed. |
| **Methods and reference practices** | Role-appropriate AI and conventional methods, matched no-AI baselines, AI-role ablations, declared feedback budgets, experimental protocols, failure analysis, and reference reporting practices. | Attribution of gains to the method, representation, tool path, or added feedback rather than to an unmatched setup. |
| **Evaluation and neutral adjudication** | Held-out tasks, cross-tool calibration, uncertainty and total-cost reporting, independent evaluation services, common challenge procedures, and documented retest or appeal paths. | Separation of method effects from private setup differences and a credible way to narrow or settle contested claims. |
| **Reporting and claim interfaces** | Compact study indexes, paper-facing claim summaries, disclosure boundaries, accepted and rejected alternatives, accountable roles, and runnable receipts when replay is claimed. | Reviewers can reconstruct the claim and distinguish context, audit, replay, and reproduction without demanding universal disclosure. |
| **Standards, governance, and community institutions** | Stable interface and evidence semantics, version ownership, integrity checks, waiver expiration, drift monitoring, revalidation, retirement, and incident-driven reopening rules. | Records and evaluations remain meaningful without freezing a particular model, method, or tool implementation too early. |
| **Incentives and confidentiality** | Publication and artifact credit, benchmark status, industrial value for neutral evaluation, protected evaluation paths, and credit for useful failure records. | Teams benefit from contributing durable evidence while respecting legitimate intellectual-property and disclosure boundaries. |
| **Education** | Courses, labs, capstones, review exercises, and mentoring aligned to the six capabilities, including mechanism testing, AI-contribution evaluation, redaction, and accountable delegation. | Architects learn to use AI across a bounded study without having to personally implement every method or tool. |

: **Field building requires a connected stack:** Data, environments, methods, evaluation, reporting, institutions, incentives, and education must reinforce one another if AI-assisted architecture work is to become comparable and cumulative. {#tbl-field-building-stack tbl-colwidths="[22,40,30]"}

Classifying the registry's 39 current entries onto these eight layers shows
where that assembly stands today (@fig-ch10-stack-coverage). Thirty-five
entries supply datasets, benchmarks, environments, and tool contracts, four
supply methods, and the five layers above them hold none, so the field is
building its instruments bottom-up while evaluation, reporting, governance,
incentives, and education remain open construction.

![**Registry coverage of the field-building stack:** The 39 entries of the living resource registry (@sec-appendix-c-architecture-20-resources) classified onto the eight layers of @tbl-field-building-stack, one primary role per entry, snapshot dated 2026-07-14. Shaded layers hold entries; greyed layers hold none yet. The classification is a one-time editorial judgment, illustrative of where shared instruments exist rather than a governed count.](images/F11b-registry-stack-coverage){#fig-ch10-stack-coverage width="100%" fig-alt="Eight-row coverage chart mapping 39 registry entries onto the eight field-building layers, drawn as a stack with datasets at the bottom and education at the top. Versioned environments and tool contracts hold 26 entries, datasets and benchmarks hold 9, and methods and reference practices hold 4, while the five layers above methods, from evaluation and neutral adjudication through education, hold zero entries and are greyed out. A bottom note states that 35 of 39 entries sit in the two lowest layers and dates the registry snapshot to 2026-07-14."}

The useful data unit is not a dump of model reasoning or an undifferentiated
collection of logs. It is a versioned study trajectory that connects a question
and baseline to represented state, candidate actions, tool observations, failed
and rejected alternatives, mechanism-level interpretation, and a bounded
decision. Logs can supply observations and provenance. They do not supply the
architecture question, the semantics of a rejection, or the scope of the claim.

The stack's layers group into four interlocking parts that exchange shared
interfaces and depend on one another in both directions
(@fig-field-building-roadmap). Data without tool contracts carry ambiguous
outcomes. Harnesses without held-out evaluation can turn setup differences into
apparent method gains. Reporting formats without credible challenge standardize
paperwork rather than evidence. Repositories preserve artifacts, while
maintained institutions define versions, comparison rules, conflicts, appeals,
and what can settle a disputed claim. Standards should stabilize interfaces and
evidence meaning after useful practices repeat, not freeze today's models or
methods before the field understands their failure modes.

![**A field advances when its shared layers reinforce one another:** Datasets and environments, methods and reference practices, evaluation and challenge, and durable institutions exchange common task, interface, evidence, and reporting semantics. Together they support AI-assisted architecture studies that are comparable, cumulative, and contestable.](images/F11a-field-building-roadmap){#fig-field-building-roadmap width="100%" fig-alt="Reciprocal four-part field-building map connecting shared foundations, methods and reference practices, evaluation and challenge, and institutional durability around common interfaces and a joint outcome of comparable, cumulative, contestable studies."}

The stack does not require raw proprietary design disclosure. Its reporting
layer can begin with bounded records that state the task, versions, exposed
interfaces, accepted and rejected candidates, available evidence, redactions,
and accountable roles. Those records let reviewers ask not only what result was
reported, but what bounded study produced it and which parts of that study are open to
inspection.

One difficult case is failure knowledge from proprietary physical-design
flows. If those records remain entirely private, outsiders cannot inspect the
evidence and future systems repeatedly encounter the same dead ends. A useful
exchange format would separate protected implementation details from a bounded
failure lesson without pretending that the public proxy reproduces the private
result.

One possible mechanism is a redacted failure record linked to an open or
synthetic proxy. When an industrial AI-assisted study rejects a candidate
because of a proprietary physical-design constraint, the study's execution mechanism
might map the failure to a public task that preserves the rejection lesson
without exposing the design rule. The public record could report the failure
relative to the mapped public task when the mapping preserves the rejection
condition. It must not imply
that the public proxy is equivalent to the proprietary physical-design result.

```{.yaml #lst-redacted-trace include="../examples/design-loop-cards/redacted-level-0.yaml" lst-cap="**Redacted Rejected-Alternative Record:** A schema-valid Level 0 context card shares a rejected design path without disclosing protected physical-design rules. It bounds the public claim but does not claim replayability."}
```

This context-only pattern (@lst-redacted-trace) shows how a study can record the
boundary of an architectural exploration while redacting absolute dimensions
and proprietary design rules. It is a valid design-loop card, not a separate
redaction schema. Its Level 0 status is deliberate. The public record names what
was attempted and what was rejected, but it does not claim that another group
can audit or replay the protected evidence. It is not a public, comparable
evidence record. Its role is diagnostic, exposing scope and omissions rather
than substituting for audit, replay, or reproduction.
The larger public view in @tbl-public-evidence-ledger names what would be needed
before treating a redacted record as comparable evidence. Some organizations
may be unable to share that much.
The point is field-level accountability, not full disclosure. Such a record
should expose what kind of evidence exists, what was redacted, who could inspect
the protected portion, and which commitment the public record can and cannot
support.

| **Field** | **Public record** | **Why it matters** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Task and workload class** | Workload family, scenario, benchmark or trace version, and excluded cases. | Prevents a result from being read as broader than the task that produced it. |
| **Action-observation interface** | What automated participants could change, what they could observe, and which tools or environments returned feedback. | Makes the exposed design space inspectable. |
| **Tool and model versions** | Simulator, compiler, EDA flow, benchmark, model, and prompt or policy versions when they affect the result. | Makes drift and reproduction failures diagnosable. |
| **Model and feedback budget** | Model calls, GPU-hours, energy or carbon exposure, tool or license cost, human review cost, and amortization basis when material. | Prevents AI assistance from making an expensive study look artificially cheap. |
| **Accepted and rejected candidates** | A compact list of advanced candidates, rejected candidates, and the rule or evidence that separated them. | Makes rejection reasons available for bounded reuse. |
| **Evidence stages** | Proxy, simulation, compiler/runtime, RTL, physical, silicon, or deployment feedback used, with fidelity limits. | Shows whether the evidence matches the claimed commitment. |
| **Telemetry coverage** | Hardware stepping, software stack, deployment version, fleet cohort, sampling policy, privacy filter, canary or rollback label, incident context, and decision owner when field evidence is used. | Prevents deployment evidence from being read as cleaner or broader than it is. |
| **Redaction and ownership** | What was hidden, why it was hidden, who may inspect it, the disclosure or release boundary, and who owned the final decision. | Keeps IP, policy, and export boundaries explicit without hiding accountability. |

: **A public evidence record should expose the bounded study, not the proprietary design:** The minimum useful record names the task, interface, versions, candidates, evidence stages, redaction boundary, and accountable decision owner. {#tbl-public-evidence-ledger tbl-colwidths="[22,34,34]"}

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Check Redaction Before Comparison"}
Before publishing or sharing a redacted study record, verify its comparison boundary. Does it hide the workload class, action-observation interface, evidence stage, or decision owner? If any of these are missing, the record can provide context but cannot be treated as comparable evidence for an AI-assisted claim.
:::

When evaluating new benchmarks against this standard, read them by the study
properties they expose, not by their names. ArchGym
[@KrishnanEtAl2023ArchGym] contributes simulator-connected search
environments, while QuArch [@PrakashEtAl2025QuArch;
@PrakashEtAl2025QuArchReasoning] contributes expert-validated architecture
knowledge and reasoning questions. CircuitNet, a dataset for machine learning
in physical design, contributes physical-design features and labels for
cross-stage prediction [@ChaiEtAl2022CircuitNet]. ChiPBench
(@sec-feedback-verification-trust) evaluates placement algorithms through final
power, performance, and area and exposes cases where intermediate metrics do
not survive the end-to-end flow [@WangEtAl2025ChiPBench].
The common test is whether an AI-assisted architecture claim can show the
question it addresses, where AI participated, what state and tools it used,
what failed, what evidence supports the result, and who owned the commitment.

This apparatus has a specific case in mind.

::: {.callout-war-story title="What the flagship leaves unsettled"}
**The claim.** Across this book, AlphaChip, a reinforcement-learning method for macro placement, has served as our concrete anchor [@MirhoseiniEtAl2021GraphPlacement; @ChengEtAl2023AssessmentRL; @GoldieEtAl2024ThatChipHasSailed]. Its strongest claims relied on proprietary pretraining on Google's own chips, which were not part of the public artifact record.

**The gap.** That missing data created a gap that could not be closed from public artifacts alone. Independent groups could not reconstruct the strongest claims, contributing to a continuing dispute over reproducibility.

**The lesson.** The goal of Architecture 2.0 is not to win that old argument, but to prevent the next one. A public evidence record and a redacted rejected-alternative record (@lst-redacted-trace, @tbl-public-evidence-ledger) can expose the scope and disclosure boundary of a study without revealing proprietary IP. Those records make a claim easier to inspect, but they cannot substitute for shared evidence or independent adjudication when a stronger public commitment requires them.
:::

The field also needs failure records, including failed runs, rejected candidates, invalid configurations,
stale benchmarks, bad proxies, tool errors, and ideas that were ruled out by
expert judgment. These records are exactly what an AI-assisted study needs if
future systems are to learn from the field's failures rather than rediscover
them. A community that records only successful artifacts gives future systems a
distorted view of architecture practice.

The goal is not to force every organization to publish internal traces. The goal
is to build schemas, examples, synthetic tasks, open benchmarks, redacted
records, and review artifacts that make credible AI-assisted study design discussable.
That is how Architecture 2.0 can become a research area rather than a set of
private demos.
That goal also needs a practical incentive. Teams are more likely to preserve useful failure records
when the record pays for itself by preventing repeated high-fidelity failures,
making handoffs cheaper, supporting review credit, and showing when an old
rejection should be retired rather than repeated forever.

### Start With One Reviewable Study

A team need not wait for the whole stack. The minimum useful target is one
reviewable study attached to a design review, design-space exploration, or
paper. It should satisfy five study checks:

- **Decision scope:** Name the task and the decision it can inform.
- **Tool path:** Connect at least one tool path to a versioned environment
  contract.
- **Run record:** Preserve successful and failed runs.
- **Evidence boundary:** State what evidence supports the survivor.
- **Decision rights:** Assign automated and accountable decision rights.

If a second person can reconstruct what was tried, what failed, what advanced
automatically, what required review, and who remains answerable, the record is
already useful. @sec-appendix-a-bootstrapping turns this minimum target into a
step-by-step bootstrap path, with five post-run checks on the execution record.

The study record also carries the lifecycle boundary. A shared failure record needs
reuse limits, revalidation rules, and a retirement path so a local rejection
does not become a false universal rule. Tacit knowledge belongs only where it
can be named as state, rationale, waiver, or owner judgment. Model-side compute
and energy cost, post-silicon telemetry, integrity records, and legal, IP, or
export-control boundaries belong whenever they affect the claim. These are not
extra fields for their own sake; they determine whether the record remains valid
when tools, workloads, models, and owners change.

Education is where the field-building agenda becomes a performance test. Shared
infrastructure matters only if architects can use it to conduct and challenge
better studies.

## What a Graduate Can Do

A curriculum for Architecture 2.0 succeeds only if learners can apply what they
learn to new architecture problems. Constructive alignment starts
with intended learning outcomes and aligns teaching activities and assessments
to the performances those outcomes require [@Biggs1996ConstructiveAlignment].
The Preface designed the book backward from one integrated test. A reader should
be able to decide where AI assistance is useful, conduct a bounded study, and
defend an evidence-limited recommendation that another architect can challenge.
@tbl-graduate-capabilities makes the corresponding performance evidence
explicit.

| **Capability** | **A graduate can** | **Performance evidence** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Formulate** | Bound an unfamiliar architecture problem with a baseline, alternatives, constraints, claim, and non-goals. | Study brief. |
| **Explore** | Choose AI or conventional methods, compare meaningful alternatives, and preserve why serious candidates failed or were rejected. | Alternative set, method rationale, and rejected-candidate record. |
| **Implement** | Produce enough executable structure and tool connectivity to test the claim. | Checkable artifact and tool contract. |
| **Evaluate** | Use representative workloads, matched architecture baselines, justified fidelity, uncertainty, counterevidence, and total feedback cost, plus a conventional or no-AI comparator when benefit from AI is claimed. | Evaluation packet. |
| **Explain** | Form and test a mechanism-level account of why the result occurred. | Mechanism hypothesis plus sensitivity, intervention, or ablation. |
| **Defend** | Make a bounded recommendation with tradeoffs, limits, ownership, and reversal conditions. | Decision memo and oral or written defense. |

: **The six capabilities end in observable performance:** A learner demonstrates Architecture 2.0 through a bounded architecture study and defensible decision, not through terminology alone. {#tbl-graduate-capabilities tbl-colwidths="[16,48,28]"}

The performance evidence is assessed as a coherent whole using the applicable
review checks below, not by artifact presence or card completion. A justified
finding that AI assistance added no value can satisfy the evaluation outcome;
the learner is not required to manufacture an AI win. The capstone should use a
problem other than the lighthouse case. The learner receives a new,
tractable architecture objective within their domain competence, together with
a feasible tool and evidence budget, and must bound the study. The final record
includes a study brief, alternative and rejection record, method rationale,
checkable artifact and tool path, evaluation packet, tested mechanism
explanation, and bounded decision memo that the learner defends under
independent challenge. @sec-running-the-loop supplies one worked example of an
iterative study turn, and the companion labs provide selected practice. The
open-ended study tests transfer across all six capabilities.

Those outcomes give reviewers a corresponding question. What should they check
in the study records and claims that learners, researchers, and practitioners
produce?

## Design Principles as Review Checks

Classic computer architecture relies on quantitative metrics and disciplined
comparison to evaluate artifacts [@HennessyPatterson2017QuantitativeApproach].
Architecture 2.0 retains that method and adds review checks for the AI-assisted
work that produces an architectural claim. Each chapter contributes one stable
principle. Together they test whether a bounded study has framed the question,
represented the work, connected methods to tools, gathered appropriate
evidence, explained the result, and assigned decision rights. They do not
replace architectural novelty or insight. They make the path to those
contributions easier to inspect (@tbl-architecture-20-design-principles).

| **Principle** | **Where it comes from** | **What to check** |
| --- | --- | --- |
| **Design the AI-assisted study, not only the generator.** | @sec-moonshot | A prompt or generated artifact is not enough. The study must connect the question, alternatives, tool path, evidence, explanation, and decision. |
| **Diagnose the loop before adding AI.** | @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales | More candidates do not help if representation, action, feedback, rejection, or commitment is the actual bound. |
| **State the claim as a review object.** | @sec-architecture-20-ontology | A result should name workload, baseline, design space, objective, constraints, evidence, rejection rule, and decision owner. |
| **Make architecture work legible to the automated loop.** | @sec-data-representations-world-models | Data must include provenance, assumptions, costs, failed runs, and rejected alternatives, not only successful endpoints. |
| **Turn tools into environments.** | @sec-architecture-environments-tool-interfaces | A wrapper is credible only when it defines legal actions, observations, costs, invalid states, and rejection paths. |
| **Match methods to roles.** | @sec-methods-generation-prediction-optimization | Choose a method for the architecture work it can improve, then compare it against suitable conventional and no-AI baselines. |
| **Escalate with commitment.** | @sec-feedback-verification-trust | Evidence requirements should rise with claim scope, rollback cost, blast radius, and the need for independent challenge. |
| **Stop at an honest evidence level.** | @sec-running-the-loop | The study should report what its evidence supports, what it rejected, and what would overturn the decision. |
| **Recognize the rejection-bound regime.** | @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack | When proposals outpace trusted dispositions, improve validated rejection coverage and adjudication capacity before adding more candidates. |
| **Keep accountable decision rights visible.** | @sec-what-architect-owns | The record must show who or what may advance, reject, escalate, or waive, and which accountable role remains answerable for consequential commitment. |

: **Architecture 2.0 design principles are review checks:** Each principle asks whether a bounded study has made enough of the architecture problem explicit for another architect to inspect, reject, or extend it. Replay and reproduction require the additional artifacts appropriate to those claims. {#tbl-architecture-20-design-principles tbl-colwidths="[28,20,42]"}

These principles are deliberately phrased as checks, not slogans. A reader
reviewing a paper can ask which principle is visible and which is missing. A
researcher building a tool can ask which principle the tool makes easier to
practice. A program committee, artifact committee, or internal review group can
apply the applicable checks to a new paper, environment, or design review.

These principles also change what counts as a mature research contribution. The
study method and claim-producing path can be part of the contribution when they
materially affect the result. Papers should then make those choices inspectable
rather than treating them as process details outside the contribution.

Where the field cannot yet apply a check reliably or compare its result across
studies, it has a research problem rather than a reporting problem.

## A Research Agenda That Outlives Today's Models

The field-building stack names shared means. The research agenda names the
questions those means should help answer. Model feature lists will age, but the
problems in @tbl-durable-research-agenda remain useful as models and tools
improve.

| **Research problem** | **What would count as progress** |
| --- | --- |
| **Architectural value and attribution** | Matched studies showing when AI improves an artifact, architectural insight, or decision under comparable tool, human, compute, and feedback budgets. |
| **Representation, environments, and transfer** | Representations and environment contracts that preserve decision-relevant state, legal actions, and failure semantics across workloads, tools, abstraction levels, and technologies, together with tests that expose where transfer fails. |
| **Evidence and calibration** | Measurements of when cheap proxies predict stronger evidence, where candidate rankings reverse, how uncertainty behaves, and when a study should escalate. |
| **Explanation and counterevidence** | AI-assisted mechanism explanations that make predictions and survive sensitivity analysis, intervention, workload shift, or adversarial challenge. |
| **Delegation and decision quality** | Controlled or field studies showing how different authority assignments affect decision quality, review burden, error detection, escalation, and overreliance. |
| **Institutions and cumulative knowledge** | Documented cases where shared or protected evaluation narrows a disputed claim, survives a version change, or transfers a failure lesson without improper disclosure. |

: **A durable agenda asks what evidence would change the field's conclusions:** Progress should improve architectural value, transfer, calibration, explanation, delegation, or cumulative knowledge rather than only expand model capability. {#tbl-durable-research-agenda tbl-colwidths="[28,62]"}

Self-improving design systems provide one demanding case. Future systems may
revise their own representations, propose new tasks, repair tool interfaces,
organize failure records, and recalibrate evidence policies. They may also adapt
toward benchmark gaming, hidden failures, tool overfitting, or biased traces.
Greater capability can change which work is delegated, but it does not make
objectives, evidence standards, exceptions, and consequential authority
self-assigning.

This matters most because the design target no longer holds still. When software
is continuously regenerated and retrained, as @sec-design-loop-no-longer-scales
argued and @sec-loop-patterns-across-stack showed at fleet scale, a specific
artifact ages as the workload it served moves on. The artifact remains the thing
committed at a point in time. The durable discipline is how the study bounds
intent, tests current evidence, rejects unsupported candidates, and reopens a
decision as the target moves.

Earlier Architecture 2.0 work framed the horizon through increasing autonomy
balanced with human expertise [@ReddiYazdanbakhsh2025Architecture20]. This book
makes that balance operational through the scope of delegated authority. Review
which transitions an automated participant may make within stated bounds,
which checks or specialists hold delegated authority, and which consequential
decision rights remain assigned to accountable people or organizational roles.
That scope, not an autonomy label, is what the architect must keep designing and
maintaining.

## The Architect's Standing Obligation

Across all six capabilities, the architect's standing obligation is to keep the
claim, delegated authority, evidence limit, and consequential owner visible.
Routine checks need not wait for manual approval, and one person need not hold
every decision right. The study must still show who or what may act, when an
exception escalates, what evidence supports the proposed consequence, and who
remains answerable when the context changes. Replay or reproduction additionally
requires the runnable inputs, environment, provenance, and receipts appropriate
to the claim.

::: {#pri-human-accountable .callout-design-principle title="Keep accountable decision rights visible"}
Checks and routine transitions may be automated or delegated. The bounded study must
still show who or what may advance, reject, escalate, or waive; which accountable
person or organizational role accepts consequential commitment; and who must
explain and revise the decision when the evidence or context changes.
:::

::: {.callout-architect-checkpoint title="Standing Obligation"}
- **Assignment test:** Can a reviewer identify the role that set scope, authorized automated checks, judged evidence sufficiency, and accepted consequential commitment?
- **Exception test:** Are escalation, waiver, rollback, and reopening paths explicit before an exception occurs?
- **Lifecycle test:** When tools, workloads, or owners change, does the record transfer or retire those decision rights rather than silently preserving stale authority?
:::

## Final Thoughts

When this book opened with a request for an entire XR subsystem from a short
text description, it may have sounded as if the computer architect's job was
ending. The argument developed across these chapters is different. AI expands
the work architects can delegate, while raising the standard for making the
resulting architecture claims inspectable and accountable.

The tools, models, and automated systems driving this shift will keep changing,
so a catalog of current capabilities will age quickly. The durable anchor is
engineering discipline. In some workflows, generating plausible candidates
will become faster and cheaper than judging them. Those workflows become
rejection-bound when proposal arrival exceeds trusted disposition capacity, and
commitment rather than artifact generation becomes scarce. Not every study is
in that regime, and candidate throughput is not the field's goal. The scarce
result is architectural understanding or judgment that the available evidence
can support.

The book's two running examples mark the distance the discipline must close.
The lighthouse prompt names the reach, a difficult architecture request whose
apparent simplicity hides workload, interface, tool, evidence, explanation,
and decision obligations. The AlphaChip war story names the methodological debt
surrounding a consequential result whose strongest public claims could not be
fully replayed or independently adjudicated from the available record. That gap
does not establish that the method was wrong. It shows why shared evaluation,
bounded disclosure, and institutions capable of credible challenge matter.

Architecture 2.0 is the engineering discipline of using AI, grounded in
architectural representations, tools, and experiments, to formulate, explore,
implement, evaluate, explain, and defend computer architecture decisions. A
design loop matters when the work iterates because it can make execution and
analysis inspectable. Cards, evidence records, checks, and receipts can support
that work. None is the destination. The field advances when architects use AI
to ask better questions, construct and test stronger alternatives, explain why
results occur, and defend decisions whose evidence and limits another architect
can challenge.
